target.name: Bastimolide A
target.motivation: Bastimolide A is a potent antimalarial (IC50 = 80-270 nM) and its complicated structure presents a synthetic challenge. The promising biological activities and unique structures of related natural products have attracted the interest of synthetic chemists. The authors aim for an efficient and scalable synthesis using catalytic reactions to reduce costs and waste, and to enable access to stereoisomers for biological and SAR studies.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: convergent approach, Type I Anion Relay Chemistry, used excess amounts of toxic heavy metals, most transformations not catalytic

success_criteria.free_text: Avoid stoichiometric asymmetric reactions or excess amounts of heavy metal reagents. Catalytically forge seven of 10 stereogenic centers.

key_disconnections.reasoning: To minimize isomerization of the Z-unsaturated lactone moiety under mild conditions.
Ring-opening reaction of epoxide 7 with alkyne 6.
Cross-metathesis reaction of enone 8 with terminal olefin 9 and a CPA-catalyzed diastereoselective allylation reaction.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis aims to construct bastimolide A from simple enantioenriched subunits (6, 7, 8, and 9) which are themselves synthesized using chiral Brønsted acid-catalyzed enantioselective allylation reactions. The strategy emphasizes catalytic reactions to reduce waste and cost, and to control stereochemistry efficiently.

thought.background: The C4-C19 fragment synthesis begins with a known CPA/CuBr-catalyzed enantioselective allylboration of TMS-substituted acetylenic aldehyde.
thought.gap: To obtain the enantioenriched propargylic alcohol with high yield and enantioselectivity, and to ensure catalyst recovery for scalability.
thought.decision: To synthesize enantioenriched propargylic alcohol 12 from 3-(trimethylsilyl)-2-propynal (10) and allylboronic reagent 11 using (S)-2 and CuBr as catalysts, expecting quantitative yield and perfect enantioselectivity, with catalyst recovery.

outcome.observation: Enantioenriched propargylic alcohol 12 was obtained in quantitative yield with perfect enantioselectivity. The CPA catalyst (S)-2 was recovered in 98% yield and reused without loss of yield or enantioselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Our synthesis commenced with the CPA/CuBr-catalyzed enantioselective allylboration of TMS-substituted acetylenic aldehyde 10 (Figure 3a).22 We obtained enantioenriched propargylic alcohol 12 in quantitative yield with perfect enantioselectivity using only 0.5 mol % of CPA. Furthermore, CPA (S)-2 used in this reaction could be recovered in 98% yield through a simple workup using N-methyl-2-aminoethanol to trap boron compounds. The recovered catalyst promoted the allylboration without losing chemical yield and enantioselectivity.38

thought.background: Propargylic alcohol 12 has been successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: To protect the secondary hydroxyl group to enable further transformations.
thought.decision: To protect the secondary hydroxyl group of 12 with a TBS group to afford terminal olefin 13.

outcome.observation: Subsequent protection of the secondary hydroxyl group by a TBS group afforded terminal olefin 13.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent protection of the secondary hydroxyl group by a TBS group afforded terminal olefin 13.

thought.gap: To transform 13 into terminal alkyne 6 through a sequence of reactions.
thought.decision: To perform olefin metathesis with methyl acrylate, ester reduction by DIBAL-H, and protection of the primary hydroxyl group accompanied by deprotection of the TMS group at the alkyne terminus to produce terminal alkyne 6.

outcome.observation: A three-step operation involving olefin metathesis with methyl acrylate, ester reduction by DIBAL-H, and protection of the primary hydroxyl group accompanied by deprotection of the TMS group at the alkyne terminus produced terminal alkyne 6 in a good yield.

evidence.text_span: A three-step operation involving olefin metathesis with methyl acrylate, ester reduction by DIBAL-H, and protection of the primary hydroxyl group accompanied by deprotection of the TMS group at the alkyne terminus produced terminal alkyne 6 in a good yield.

thought.background: Subunit epoxide 7 is needed for the C4-C19 fragment synthesis.
thought.gap: To synthesize epoxide 7 from aldehyde 16 efficiently and enantioselectively.
thought.decision: To prepare epoxide subunit 7 from aldehyde 16 in a four-step sequence, including CPA-catalyzed allylboration and Bartlett-Smith epoxide synthesis.

outcome.observation: Epoxide subunit 7 could be readily prepared from aldehyde 16 in a four-step sequence, including the enantioselective CPA-catalyzed allylboration and the Bartlett−Smith epoxide synthesis.

evidence.text_span: As shown in Figure 3b, epoxide subunit 7 could be readily prepared from aldehyde 16 in a four-step sequence, including the enantioselective CPA-catalyzed allylboration and the Bartlett−Smith epoxide synthesis.39,40

thought.background: Terminal alkyne 6 and epoxide 7 are available.
thought.gap: To couple these two fragments and protect the newly formed hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To deprotonate terminal alkyne 6 with n-BuLi, followed by addition of epoxide 7 in the presence of BF3·Et2O to promote ring-opening, and then protect the generated secondary hydroxyl group with TBSCl.

outcome.observation: Deprotonation of the terminal alkyne by n-BuLi, followed by the addition of enantioenriched epoxide 7 in the presence of BF3·Et2O, promoted the ring-opening reaction of the epoxide, affording the coupling product in excellent yield. The protection of the generated secondary hydroxyl group was also successful.

evidence.text_span: Next, deprotonation of the terminal alkyne by n-BuLi, followed by the addition of enantioenriched epoxide 7 in the presence of BF3·Et2O,41 promoted the ring-opening reaction of the epoxide, affording the coupling product in excellent yield. The protection of the generated secondary hydroxyl group and the reduction of the double and triple bonds, accompanied by benzyl group removal under mild hydrogenation conditions, gave the primary alcohol 15.

thought.background: The coupled and protected intermediate 14 is available.
thought.gap: To reduce the double and triple bonds and remove the benzyl protecting group.
thought.decision: To perform hydrogenation of 14 to reduce the double and triple bonds and remove the benzyl group, yielding primary alcohol 15.

outcome.observation: Reduction of the double and triple bonds, accompanied by benzyl group removal under mild hydrogenation conditions, gave the primary alcohol 15.

evidence.text_span: The protection of the generated secondary hydroxyl group and the reduction of the double and triple bonds, accompanied by benzyl group removal under mild hydrogenation conditions, gave the primary alcohol 15.

thought.gap: To introduce a terminal olefin and then oxidize the primary alcohol to an aldehyde, while selectively deprotecting the primary TBS group.
thought.decision: To introduce a terminal olefin via Grieco-Nishizawa olefination, selectively deprotect the primary TBS group, and then perform TEMPO oxidation to furnish aldehyde 4 (C4-C19 fragment).

outcome.observation: A terminal olefin was introduced by the Grieco−Nishizawa olefination of 15. The selective deprotection of the primary TBS group and the subsequent TEMPO oxidation furnished the C4−C19 fragment, namely, aldehyde 4, from commercially available 3-(trimethylsilyl)-2-propynal (10) in 11 steps and a total yield of 41.2%.

evidence.text_span: A terminal olefin was introduced by the Grieco−Nishizawa olefination42 of 15. The selective deprotection of the primary TBS group and the subsequent TEMPO oxidation furnished the C4−C19 fragment, namely, aldehyde 4, from commercially available 3-(trimethylsilyl)-2-propynal (10) in 11 steps and a total yield of 41.2%.

thought.background: The C20-C43 fragment synthesis begins with an enantioselective allylation of pivalaldehyde (18) with allylboronic reagent 11.
thought.gap: To achieve high enantioselectivity in the allylation of pivalaldehyde, which is sterically hindered.
thought.decision: To use SPINOL-derived catalyst (R)-2, which generally gives the best results in conventional enantioselective allylboration with alkyl aldehydes, for the reaction with pivalaldehyde.

outcome.observation: The desired product was obtained in good yield but with poor enantioselectivity (40% ee).

evidence.text_span: The construction of 5 started with a chiral phosphoramidecatalyzed enantioselective allylation of pivalaldehyde (18) with allylboronic reagent 11. In our initial attempt, we used SPINOL-derived catalyst (R)-2, which generally gives the best results in the conventional enantioselective allylboration reaction with alkyl aldehydes,21,43 for the reaction with pivalaldehyde. The desired product was obtained in good yield but with poor enantioselectivity (40% ee).44

thought.background: The previous attempt with catalyst (R)-2 resulted in poor enantioselectivity for the allylation of pivalaldehyde.
thought.gap: To achieve excellent enantioselectivity for the allylation of pivalaldehyde.
thought.decision: To use phosphoramide catalyst (S)-3, which can promote multiple interactions with substrates, for the allylation of pivalaldehyde (18) with allylboronic reagent 11, expecting quantitative yield and excellent enantioselectivity, with catalyst recovery and scalability.

outcome.observation: When phosphoramide catalyst (S)-3 was used, the desired product 19 was obtained in quantitative yield with excellent enantioselectivity. The reaction proceeded smoothly with only 0.5 mol % of catalyst, which could be recovered in 99% yield and reused. More than 2.5 g (20 mmol) of enantioenriched homoallylic alcohol 19 was prepared using only 44 mg of the catalyst.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, when we used phosphoramide catalyst (S)-3, which can promote multiple interactions with substrates for this reaction, the desired product was obtained in quantitative yield with excellent enantioselectivity.24 The reaction proceeded smoothly with only 0.5 mol % of catalyst in pentane, and the catalyst could be recovered in 99% yield and used again in the same reaction to give the desired product without any loss of chemical yield and enantioselectivity. Finally, more than 2.5 g (20 mmol) of enantioenriched homoallylic alcohol 19 was prepared using only 44 mg of the catalyst.

thought.gap: To protect the hydroxyl group and then perform cross-metathesis to extend the carbon chain.
thought.decision: To treat alcohol 19 with BzCl and pyridine, followed by cross-metathesis with crotonaldehyde using Hoveyda−Grubbs II catalyst, to give the corresponding α,β-unsaturated aldehyde.

outcome.observation: Treatment of alcohol 19 with BzCl and pyridine, followed by cross-metathesis with crotonaldehyde using Hoveyda−Grubbs II catalyst, gave the corresponding α,β-unsaturated aldehyde in good yield. The obtained aldehyde was converted into homoallylic alcohol 9 via an asymmetric allylboration using (S)-2 in a highly diastereoselective fashion without the influence of the Lewis basic benzoate moiety and the stereogenic center at C39.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of alcohol 19 with BzCl and pyridine, followed by crossmetathesis with crotonaldehyde using Hoveyda−Grubbs II catalyst, gave the corresponding α,β-unsaturated aldehyde in good yield. The obtained aldehyde was converted into homoallylic alcohol 9 via an asymmetric allylboration using (S)-2 in a highly diastereoselective fashion without the influence of the Lewis basic benzoate moiety and the stereogenic center at C39.

thought.gap: To synthesize subunit enone 8 from 23 through a sequence of reactions.
thought.decision: To perform CPA-catalyzed allylboration of 23, followed by TBS protection to give silyl ether 24. Then, Ir-catalyzed hydroboration of 24 to furnish alkylboronic ester 25, which will be used in Aggarwal’s three-carbon homologation to provide subunit 8 on a gram scale.

outcome.observation: CPA-catalyzed allylboration followed by TBS protection gave silyl ether 24 in 98% yield with 95% ee. The Ir-catalyzed hydroboration of 24 furnished alkylboronic ester 25, which was subsequently used in Aggarwal’s three-carbon homologation reaction to provide subunit 8 on a gram scale.

evidence.text_span: Next, we synthesized subunit enone 8 from readily available NAP-protected aldehyde 23 (Figure 4b). CPA-catalyzed allylboration followed by TBS protection gave silyl ether 24 in 98% yield with 95% ee. The Ir-catalyzed hydroboration of 24 furnished alkylboronic ester 25, which was subsequently used in Aggarwal’s three-carbon homologation45 reaction to provide subunit 8 on a gram scale.

thought.background: Silylated subunit 9 and subunit enone 8 are available.
thought.gap: To unite these subunits and then reduce the enone to introduce a stereocenter.
thought.decision: To unite silylated subunit 9 with subunit enone 8 via olefin cross-metathesis using Hoveyda−Grubbs II catalyst to afford enone 20. Then, expose enone 20 to BH3·Me2S in the presence of (S)-oxazaborolidine (CBS catalyst) to form the stereocenter at C31 with high diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Silylated subunit 9 was united with subunit enone 8 via olefin cross-metathesis using Hoveyda−Grubbs II catalyst to afford desired enone 20 in high yield. Exposing this enone to BH3·Me2S in the presence of (S)-oxazaborolidine (CBS catalyst) resulted in the formation of the stereocenter at C31 with high diastereoselectivity (dr = 15:1 dr). Protection of the generated secondary alcohol by a TBS group followed by hydrogenation of olefins involving deprotection by the NAP group afforded primary alcohol 21.

evidence.text_span: Silylated subunit 9 was united with subunit enone 8 via olefin cross-metathesis using Hoveyda−Grubbs II catalyst to afford desired enone 20 in high yield. Exposing this enone to BH3·Me2S in the presence of (S)- oxazaborolidine (CBS catalyst) resulted in the formation of the stereocenter at C31 with high diastereoselectivity (dr = 15:1 dr). Protection of the generated secondary alcohol by a TBS group followed by hydrogenation of olefins involving deprotection by the NAP group afforded primary alcohol 21.

thought.gap: To oxidize the primary alcohol to an aldehyde and then extend the carbon chain with stereocontrol.
thought.decision: To perform TEMPO oxidation of 21 to generate aldehyde 22. Then, convert 22 into C20−C43 fragment 5 through a three-step transformation: CPA-catalyzed asymmetric allylboration, PMB protection, and modified Wacker oxidation.

outcome.observation: After the TEMPO oxidation of 21, generated aldehyde 22 was converted into C20−C43 fragment 5 through a three-step transformation: CPA-catalyzed asymmetric allylboration, PMB protection, and the modified Wacker oxidation. To our delight, the CPA-catalyzed allylation reaction of functionalized aldehyde 22 gave the corresponding product with excellent diastereoselectivity, despite having a benzoyl group that might interact with CPA and four stereocenters that might promote mismatch interactions with the catalyst. The overall yield for preparing C20−C43 fragment 5 was 42.4% in 13 steps from pivalaldehyde (18).

evidence.text_span: After the TEMPO oxidation of 21, generated aldehyde 22 was converted into C20−C43 fragment 5 through a three-step transformation: CPA-catalyzed asymmetric allylboration, PMB protection, and the modified Wacker oxidation.46 To our delight, the CPAcatalyzed allylation reaction of functionalized aldehyde 22 gave the corresponding product with excellent diastereoselectivity, despite having a benzoyl group that might interact with CPA and four stereocenters that might promote mismatch interactions with the catalyst. The overall yield for preparing C20−C43 fragment 5 was 42.4% in 13 steps from pivalaldehyde (18).

thought.background: C4−C19 fragment 4 and C20−C43 fragment 5 are available in ample quantities.
thought.gap: To assemble these fragments through a diastereoselective aldol reaction.
thought.decision: To treat 5 with Cy2BCl and Et3N to furnish the corresponding boryl enolate, then add aldehyde 4 to give the desired aldol adduct 26.

outcome.observation: Treatment of 5 with Cy2BCl and Et3N furnished the corresponding boryl enolate. Adding aldehyde 4 gave the desired aldol adduct in 94% yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr = >20:1).

evidence.text_span: With ample quantities of C4−C19 fragment 4 and C20− C43 fragment 5, we assembled these fragments through a diastereoselective aldol reaction (Figure 5). Treatment of 5 with Cy2BCl and Et3N furnished the corresponding boryl enolate. Adding aldehyde 4 gave the desired aldol adduct in 94% yield with excellent diastereoselectivity (dr = >20:1).47−49

thought.gap: To reduce the ketone and protect the resulting diol to form intermediate 27 with complete stereogenic centers.
thought.decision: To reduce ketone 26 under Evans−Saksena conditions to give a 1,3-anti diol, then protect the diol using TBSOTf and lutidine to afford intermediate 27.

outcome.observation: Reduction of ketone 26 under Evans−Saksena conditions gave a 1,3-anti diol in good yield. The diol was subjected to protection using TBSOTf and lutidine to afford important intermediate 27 having complete stereogenic centers in gram quantities.

evidence.text_span: Reduction of ketone 26 under Evans−Saksena conditions gave a 1,3-anti diol in good yield. The diol was subjected to protection using TBSOTf and lutidine to afford important intermediate 27 having complete stereogenic centers in gram quantities.

thought.gap: To selectively remove the Bz group and install the C1−C3 unit to form ester 29, which contains all carbons of bastimolide A.
thought.decision: To selectively remove the Bz group by reacting with DIBAL-H at −78 °C, followed by installation of the C1−C3 unit using acid anhydride 28 under mild basic conditions, to provide ester 29.

outcome.observation: Selective removal of the Bz group by reacting with DIBAL-H at −78 °C (98% yield), followed by installation of the C1−C3 unit by using acid anhydride 28 under mild basic conditions, provided ester 29 having all of the carbons of bastimolide A (1).

evidence.text_span: Selective removal of the Bz group by reacting with DIBAL-H at −78 °C (98%), followed by installation of the C1−C3 unit by using acid anhydride 28 under mild basic conditions, provided ester 29 having all of the carbons of bastimolide A (1).

thought.background: Ester 29 is available, containing all carbons of bastimolide A.
thought.gap: To construct the 40-membered-ring system via intramolecular Suzuki-Miyaura coupling without isomerization of the Z-olefin.
thought.decision: To use Aggarwal’s conditions for the synthesis of bastimolide B for the intramolecular Suzuki-Miyaura coupling.

outcome.observation: We initially attempted to use Aggarwal’s conditions for the synthesis of bastimolide B but obtained a low yield (ca. 10%) because the iodide was eliminated, generating the corresponding triple bond competitively.

evidence.text_span: We initially attempted to use Aggarwal’s conditions50 for the synthesis of bastimolide B but obtained a low yield (ca. 10%) because the iodide was eliminated, generating the corresponding triple bond competitively (see SI for details).

thought.background: The previous attempt at intramolecular Suzuki-Miyaura coupling resulted in low yield due to competitive iodide elimination.
thought.gap: To achieve efficient macrolactone formation without Z- to E-olefin isomerization.
thought.decision: To carefully investigate and modify the reaction conditions for the intramolecular Suzuki-Miyaura coupling to obtain macrolactone 30 without isomerization from Z-olefin to E-olefin.

outcome.observation: After carefully investigating the reaction conditions, we obtained macrolactone 30 in 61% yield without any isomerization from Z-olefin to E-olefin under the modified conditions.

evidence.text_span: After carefully investigating the reaction conditions, we obtained macrolactone 30 in 61% yield without any isomerization from Z-olefin to E-olefin under the modified conditions.51,52

thought.gap: To globally deprotect the macrolactone to yield bastimolide A.
thought.decision: To perform global deprotection of 30 by treating with DDQ in pH 7 phosphate buffer and exposing the resulting mono alcohol to aqueous HF to furnish bastimolide A (1).

outcome.observation: Finally, the global deprotection of 30 was performed by treating with DDQ in pH 7 phosphate buffer and exposing the resulting mono alcohol to aqueous HF to furnish bastimolide A (1) in 80% yield (two steps). The 1H NMR, 13C NMR, HRMS, and optical rotation data of synthesized 1 are identical to those of the natural product and Smith’s synthetic 37.

evidence.text_span: Finally, the global deprotection of 30 was performed by treating with DDQ in pH 7 phosphate buffer and exposing the resulting mono alcohol to aqueous HF to furnish bastimolide A (1) in 80% yield (two steps). The 1H NMR, 13C NMR, HRMS, and optical rotation data of synthesized 1 are identical to those of the natural product and Smith’s synthetic 37.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The longest linear sequence of the synthesis.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The synthesis is more efficient and scalable than Smith's prior synthesis (20 steps, 0.65% total yield), avoiding excess toxic heavy metals and stoichiometric asymmetric reactions. This synthesis incorporates catalytic reactions in 10 of 21 steps.
validation.significance: This total synthesis of bastimolide A is efficient and scalable, achieving a 15.4% total yield over 21 linear steps. It highlights the utility of chiral Brønsted acid-catalyzed enantio- and diastereoselective allylborations, forging seven of 10 stereogenic centers catalytically. The method for recovering phosphoramide catalyst (S)-3 and CPA (S)-2 contributes to scalability and sustainability, enabling multigram scale production of bastimolide A with reduced chemical waste and without stoichiometric chiral sources or heavy metals. This approach facilitates the synthesis of derivatives for biological and SAR studies.

source.title: Scalable Total Synthesis of Bastimolide A Enabled by Asymmetric Allylborations Catalyzed by Chiral Brønsted Acids.
source.authors: Umemiya S, Shinagawa N, Fujimoto A, Terada M.
